issues.description: FR-3e 'Mine' filter does not actually filter. When no engineer query param is set (the 'mine' default), get_loops_for_engineer(None, false, true) returns ALL loops in both Memory and Postgres implementations. The label 'mine' is rendered but no filtering occurs. FR-3e says 'Mine scopes to the viewer's own loops (derived from API-key → engineer mapping)' but no such mapping exists. With the single shared API key (FR-3g), 'Mine' and 'Team' show identical results.
issues.suggestion: Either (a) add engineer identity to the auth session (e.g., store engineer name in a second cookie at login time, or accept an engineer name field on the login form), or (b) document 'Mine' as non-functional in v1 and default the landing view to 'Team' + 'Active' instead of pretending to filter.

issues.description: FR-7b requires '<10 KB minified' for dashboard.js. The file is ~21 KB of unminified source, and there is no minification step in the build. Since assets are embedded via include_str! and served directly, the deployed file is the unminified source. Even with gzip this likely exceeds the budget. The file header comment claims '<10 KB minified' but no minification occurs.
issues.suggestion: Add a build-time minification step (e.g., a build.rs that minifies the JS before embedding), or accept the current size and update the spec/comment. Alternatively, trim the JS — some functions (initStatsFocus, initFeedFilterPersistence, initWindowToggle) are relatively small but add up.

issues.description: specs_page uses get_terminal_loops which only returns terminal loops (CONVERGED, FAILED, CANCELLED, HARDENED, SHIPPED). FR-13a says 'all past loops that ran on that spec' — an actively running loop for the same spec won't appear in the history table until it terminates. If an engineer is debugging why a spec keeps failing and one is currently running, they can't see it on this page.
issues.suggestion: Use get_loops_for_engineer with a spec_path filter (or add a spec_path param to get_loops_for_engineer) to include active loops. Alternatively, accept this as intentional and clarify that the page shows only completed runs.

issues.description: fetch_feed_items_with_engineers calls get_terminal_loops(None, None, None, None, 10_000) to extract the engineer list for filter chips on EVERY feed page load. For deployments with thousands of historical terminal loops, this fetches up to 10,000 rows just to extract distinct engineer names. The actual feed items are fetched separately with proper cursor pagination. This is wasteful and could violate FR-12d's '<1 second on mobile' target.
issues.suggestion: Add a get_distinct_engineers() method to StateStore that runs SELECT DISTINCT engineer FROM loops WHERE state IN (...terminal states...) — a much lighter query. Or cache the engineer list alongside the stats cache with a similar TTL.

issues.description: get_loops_for_engineer has LIMIT 10000 (up from 100). The dashboard's fleet summary (FR-9), stats (FR-14), and compute_stats all depend on this returning comprehensive data. For deployments with >10,000 historical loops, aggregates will silently show incomplete data. This is a soft cap that could cause subtle inaccuracies.
issues.suggestion: For dashboard aggregation queries, either remove the LIMIT or use the new get_terminal_loops which has a configurable limit parameter. The 10000 cap is fine for the card grid but not for statistical aggregation.

issues.description: Secure cookie flag is inferred from bind_addr (loopback vs non-loopback). A server bound to 0.0.0.0 behind Tailscale without TLS termination would set Secure, preventing cookie delivery over plain HTTP. This was flagged in rounds 3 and 4 but remains. It's unlikely to cause problems in practice since Tailscale typically provides TLS, but is fragile.
issues.suggestion: Add an explicit config option (e.g., dashboard_secure_cookie: bool in NautiloopConfig) or default to no Secure flag with a comment explaining that Tailscale provides transport security.

issues.description: MemoryStateStore::get_rounds_for_loops uses loop_ids.contains() which is O(m) per round, making the overall complexity O(n*m). For large datasets this is quadratic. Only affects tests (production uses Postgres with ANY($1)).
issues.suggestion: Convert loop_ids to a HashSet before iterating rounds for O(1) lookup per round.

issues.description: Trend arrow semantics are correct but potentially confusing: ↑ always means 'value increased numerically', and color (green/red) indicates favorability. For cost, ↑ is red (bad). For converge rate, ↑ is green (good). For avg rounds, ↑ is red (bad). This is internally consistent but requires users to parse both arrow direction AND color to understand the trend. A simpler UX would use only color without arrows, or only arrows pointing in the 'good' direction.
issues.suggestion: Consider adding a tooltip (title attribute) on each trend span explaining the direction, e.g., 'Cost increased by $1.20 vs prior week'. This helps mobile users who can long-press for tooltips.
